meta.title: Babel Bridge — the spectra registry (pre-authored prism blocks, MVP set)
meta.spectrum: Canonical 5-color spectrum, LOCKED: plain (⚪ white light, default) · barstool (🔴) · timbit (🟡) · boardroom (🔵) · swiss (⬜). picture (🟢) reserved v2 — never authored here.
law: Real data only — every register is derived from the named source; no facts are invented anywhere in this file.

Verbatim-locked canon (locked: true) is NEVER re-voiced: the plain register carries the sacred line untouched; every other color is a framing expansion that quotes the line exactly — never rewrites, never compresses. Locked timbit registers are verbatim fragments of the line itself.
Computed states (OVERDUE, roll-ups, counts) are never asserted in prose — dates are stated, the renderer does the math.
All spectra are agent-authored at seed time and shipped for Tim's review — nothing is ever generated client-side (the static app has no API key and never will).
Voice contracts: barstool = Tim's plain-spoken warmth, blunt with care; timbit = 3–10 word compressed bite; boardroom = professional-calm executive narrative; swiss = neutral institutional precision.

sources.cards: data/cards/pawn-princess-pp01.json · seba-hub-occupancy.json · door-to-door.json (vivid_vision + wiifm fields, verbatim)
sources.ptk: data/ptk-seed.json (the 10 real seed promises — promise text verbatim in plain)
sources.doctrine: doctrine.html core lines (verbatim chrome lines, BLUEPRINT §2.2; swiss registers reuse the page's reviewed Swiss Protocol pilot prose where it exists)
sources.timbits: data/timbits.json core[] — mapped field-for-field: quote→plain (locked), barstool→barstool (Raw Tim mode), context→boardroom (Culture Agent mode), structured derivation→swiss (Swiss Protocol mode, same shape culture.html renders), timbit→timbit (the cliché / bumper-sticker).

meta.review: Authored by the prism-core builder from the named sources; pending Tim's review (ship-it-ugly: seed now, review on the card).
meta.keys: Key convention (LAW, matches the consumers): card:<cardFile>:vv | card:<cardFile>:wiifm (card.html header) · ptk:<promiseId> (ptk.html + the card PTK centre square) · doc-* (doctrine.html core lines) · tm-* (the 25 timbits, culture.html fast-follow).

card:pawn-princess-pp01:vv.title: Vivid Vision — Pawn Princess PP-01
card:pawn-princess-pp01:vv.plain: Purpose: “Dignified cash when life happens.” BHAG: “50 dignified stores in 5 years.” Values: Dignity · Speed · Transparency.
card:pawn-princess-pp01:vv.barstool: Somebody's month just broke — a flat tire, a short paycheque — and they need cash today without being made to feel small about it. That's the whole vision: “Dignified cash when life happens.” And the big goal is exactly what it says: “50 dignified stores in 5 years” — fifty places that run on dignity, speed, and transparency.
card:pawn-princess-pp01:vv.boardroom: Pawn Princess store PP-01 operates under a declared Vivid Vision. Purpose, verbatim: “Dignified cash when life happens.” The stated BHAG — “50 dignified stores in 5 years” — is protected by three core values: Dignity, Speed, and Transparency. Every square on the store's Dingo Card is expected to serve that line.
card:pawn-princess-pp01:vv.swiss: Record: Vivid Vision — Pawn Princess PP-01. Purpose (verbatim): “Dignified cash when life happens.” BHAG (verbatim): “50 dignified stores in 5 years.” Core values: Dignity; Speed; Transparency. Caller: Lauren (store manager). Status: verbatim-locked — framing may expand, the lines never change.

meta.source: data/cards/pawn-princess-pp01.json → vivid_vision

card:pawn-princess-pp01:wiifm.title: WIIFM — Pawn Princess PP-01
card:pawn-princess-pp01:wiifm.plain: Run a balanced, trusted store — and get home on time.
card:pawn-princess-pp01:wiifm.barstool: Here's what's in it for Lauren: the drawer balances, nobody's chasing a short deposit, the store has a good name in town — and she's not stuck there till nine fixing paperwork. Balanced, trusted, home on time. That's the deal.
card:pawn-princess-pp01:wiifm.timbit: Balanced drawer. Trusted store. Home on time.
card:pawn-princess-pp01:wiifm.boardroom: For the store manager, the value proposition is operational: a store that reconciles cleanly, holds customer trust, and closes on schedule — accountability delivered without overtime.
card:pawn-princess-pp01:wiifm.swiss: Beneficiary: store manager (Dingo Caller — Lauren). Stated benefits: (1) balanced daily reconciliation; (2) sustained customer trust; (3) on-time close. Source: WIIFM line, card pawn-princess-pp01.

meta.source: data/cards/pawn-princess-pp01.json → wiifm

card:seba-hub-occupancy:vv.title: Vivid Vision — Seba Hub Occupancy Engine
card:seba-hub-occupancy:vv.plain: Purpose: “Fill what we already have — no empty night, no empty seat.” BHAG: “Every weekend a wedding, every cabin full, every season alive at Seba Hub.” Values: Hospitality · Stewardship · Dignity.
card:seba-hub-occupancy:vv.barstool: The cabins are built. The domes are built. The wedding venue is sitting right there. The vision isn't to build more — it's “Fill what we already have — no empty night, no empty seat.” The big goal says it plain: “Every weekend a wedding, every cabin full, every season alive at Seba Hub.” Hospitality, stewardship, dignity — that's how it gets filled.
card:seba-hub-occupancy:vv.boardroom: The Seba Hub Occupancy Engine's declared purpose, verbatim: “Fill what we already have — no empty night, no empty seat.” Its BHAG: “Every weekend a wedding, every cabin full, every season alive at Seba Hub.” The card's values — Hospitality, Stewardship, Dignity — govern every outreach the engine drafts; existing capacity is treated as perishable inventory.
card:seba-hub-occupancy:vv.swiss: Record: Vivid Vision — Seba Hub Occupancy Engine. Purpose (verbatim): “Fill what we already have — no empty night, no empty seat.” BHAG (verbatim): “Every weekend a wedding, every cabin full, every season alive at Seba Hub.” Core values: Hospitality; Stewardship; Dignity. Caller: Casey. Status: verbatim-locked.

meta.source: data/cards/seba-hub-occupancy.json → vivid_vision

card:seba-hub-occupancy:wiifm.title: WIIFM — Seba Hub Occupancy Engine
card:seba-hub-occupancy:wiifm.plain: Rep: warm intros to people already looking. Seba: perishable capacity stops leaking — empty nights become revenue.
card:seba-hub-occupancy:wiifm.barstool: Two people win here. The rep gets warm intros — folks who already raised their hand, not cold doors. And Seba stops watching empty cabins burn money, because an empty night is revenue you never get back. It expires at sunrise.
card:seba-hub-occupancy:wiifm.boardroom: The incentive structure serves both parties: representatives receive qualified, warm introductions to prospects already in-market, while Seba Hub converts perishable unsold capacity — nights that expire worthless — into booked revenue.
card:seba-hub-occupancy:wiifm.swiss: Beneficiaries: (1) sales representative — warm, pre-qualified introductions; (2) Seba Hub — recovery of perishable capacity (unsold nights → bookings). Source: WIIFM line, card seba-hub-occupancy.

meta.source: data/cards/seba-hub-occupancy.json → wiifm

card:door-to-door:vv.title: Vivid Vision — Door-to-Door Occupancy Drive (superseded draft)
card:door-to-door:vv.plain: Purpose: “Every unit full, every season — dignity through occupancy.” BHAG (superseded draft): 85% occupancy, daily, across every site. Values: Dignity · Hustle · Honesty.
card:door-to-door:vv.barstool: The early draft said it straight: “Every unit full, every season — dignity through occupancy.” Eighty-five percent occupancy, every day, every site — powered by dignity, hustle, and honesty. This card's been superseded — the Seba Hub Occupancy Engine carries the plan now — but the purpose line still holds up.
card:door-to-door:vv.boardroom: The Door-to-Door Occupancy Drive draft declared its purpose, verbatim: “Every unit full, every season — dignity through occupancy,” targeting 85% daily occupancy across every site under three values: Dignity, Hustle, Honesty. The card is retained as a superseded draft; its successor is the Seba Hub Occupancy Engine.
card:door-to-door:vv.swiss: Record: Vivid Vision — Door-to-Door Occupancy Drive. Purpose (verbatim): “Every unit full, every season — dignity through occupancy.” BHAG target: 85% occupancy, daily, all sites. Core values: Dignity; Hustle; Honesty. Caller: TBD. Status: SUPERSEDED — successor card: seba-hub-occupancy. Verbatim-locked.

meta.source: data/cards/door-to-door.json → vivid_vision (superseded; the BHAG's legacy site name is rendered per the locked vocabulary — Seba Hub, never the legacy spelling)

card:door-to-door:wiifm.title: WIIFM — Door-to-Door Occupancy Drive (superseded draft)
card:door-to-door:wiifm.plain: Rep: a warm list + a script that works = you earn well. Site: full rooms, steady revenue, no empty-season panic.
card:door-to-door:wiifm.barstool: For the rep it's simple: a warm list and a script that actually works means you actually earn. For the site it's fuller rooms and steady money — no white-knuckle empty season. (Early draft — the Seba Hub Occupancy Engine runs this play now.)
card:door-to-door:wiifm.timbit: Warm list. Working script. Full rooms.
card:door-to-door:wiifm.boardroom: The draft proposed aligned incentives: representatives earn well from a warm lead list and a proven script, while each site gains occupancy stability and predictable revenue. Retained as a superseded draft — the plan now lives in the Seba Hub Occupancy Engine.
card:door-to-door:wiifm.swiss: Beneficiaries: (1) representative — earnings via qualified list and script; (2) site — occupancy and revenue stability. Status: superseded — successor card: seba-hub-occupancy. Source: WIIFM line, card door-to-door.

meta.source: data/cards/door-to-door.json → wiifm

doc-intelligence-brilliance.title: Doctrine — intelligence vs brilliance
doc-intelligence-brilliance.plain: The agents bring the intelligence. The HB1000 brings the brilliance.
doc-intelligence-brilliance.barstool: The agents are smart — scary smart, around the clock. But smart isn't the same as brilliant. The judgment, the taste, the call that actually matters? That's the human. “The agents bring the intelligence. The HB1000 brings the brilliance.”
doc-intelligence-brilliance.boardroom: The fleet contributes capability at scale — research, drafting, analysis, monitoring. The human contributes what boards actually rely on: judgment, vision, and final authority. The maxim, quoted exactly: “The agents bring the intelligence. The HB1000 brings the brilliance.”
doc-intelligence-brilliance.swiss: Division of responsibility, stated formally: the agent fleet supplies analytical and executional capacity; the human principal supplies judgment, vision, and final authority. Operating maxim, verbatim: “The agents bring the intelligence. The HB1000 brings the brilliance.”

meta.source: doctrine.html verbatim chrome line; swiss register reuses the page's reviewed Swiss Protocol pilot prose

doc-8020.title: Doctrine — the 80/20 Rule
doc-8020.plain: Let the agents swing the hammers. The HB1000 holds the blueprint.
doc-8020.barstool: Eighty-twenty isn't a slogan, it's a tripwire: the agents carry at least 80% of the load, and the moment the human's share creeps past 20, the bar turns red and the Council asks why. “Let the agents swing the hammers. The HB1000 holds the blueprint.”
doc-8020.boardroom: The 80/20 covenant allocates execution to the agent fleet — a minimum 80% share — and reserves for the human principal the work only human brilliance can do: vision, values, sensitive gates, sign-off. Breaches surface automatically: a human share above 20% turns the split bar red for Council triage. Maxim, verbatim: “Let the agents swing the hammers. The HB1000 holds the blueprint.”
doc-8020.swiss: Execution is delegated to the agent fleet; architectural control and accountability remain with the human principal. Covenant threshold: human share ≤ 20%, breach state computed at render. Operating maxim, verbatim: “Let the agents swing the hammers. The HB1000 holds the blueprint.”

meta.source: doctrine.html §1; swiss register extends the page's reviewed Swiss Protocol pilot prose

doc-lanes.title: Doctrine — Authority Lanes
doc-lanes.plain: Brilliance can suggest anywhere. Authority can dispatch only in-lane.
doc-lanes.barstool: Anybody can spot a problem anywhere — say it, flag it, suggest the fix. But you only get to send agents into your own lane. Outside your lane it becomes a suggestion the lane owner can take or leave. “Brilliance can suggest anywhere. Authority can dispatch only in-lane.”
doc-lanes.boardroom: The authority-lane rule separates insight from dispatch rights: any HB1000 may raise a Fix-It Suggestion anywhere in the organization, but agent dispatch is restricted to the suggester's own lane — out-of-lane items route to the responsible owner's approval queue, protecting against accidental overreach. Verbatim: “Brilliance can suggest anywhere. Authority can dispatch only in-lane.”
doc-lanes.swiss: Rule: suggestion rights are universal; dispatch rights are lane-scoped. Out-of-lane observations generate a Fix-It Suggestion routed to the lane owner. Authority status per ticket: in_lane · suggestion_only · admin_review. Maxim, verbatim: “Brilliance can suggest anywhere. Authority can dispatch only in-lane.”

meta.source: doctrine.html §2

doc-two-shop.title: Doctrine — the Two-Shop Test
doc-two-shop.plain: The key is independence, not a fixed model brand.
doc-two-shop.barstool: Same as your car: you don't bet serious money on one mechanic's opinion. Two independent diagnoses — Canadian Tire AND the trusted local garage — then compare where they agree, where they fight, and what each one's afraid of. “The key is independence, not a fixed model brand.”
doc-two-shop.boardroom: Before serious work is dispatched, the Two-Shop Test requires two independent diagnoses — different models, or separately prompted specialists — with the agreement and disagreement analysis shown to the human before the dispatch button enables. As the doctrine states: “The key is independence, not a fixed model brand.”
doc-two-shop.swiss: Control: two_shop_test.required = true on qualifying tickets. Inputs: shop A and shop B diagnoses, independently produced. Outputs: agreement[], disagreement[], recommended_repair_path. Gate: the disagreement analysis precedes dispatch approval. Principle, verbatim: “The key is independence, not a fixed model brand.”

meta.source: doctrine.html §3

doc-baseline.title: Doctrine — baseline, never final
doc-baseline.plain: Every output is a baseline, never final.
doc-baseline.barstool: Nothing we ship is the last word — it's the starting line for the next 1%. Today's 78 is tomorrow's baseline, and somebody — human or agent — is going to beat it. “Every output is a baseline, never final.”
doc-baseline.boardroom: Standing Order #7 in operation: each deliverable is logged with a score against a declared baseline and treated as the floor for the next improvement cycle, never as a finished ceiling. Verbatim: “Every output is a baseline, never final.”
doc-baseline.swiss: Provision: Standing Order #7. Effect: all outputs enter the ledger as dated, scored baselines; improvement candidates are measured as lift against baseline. Verbatim: “Every output is a baseline, never final.”

meta.source: doctrine.html §5 / Standing Order #7

doc-ship-ugly.title: Doctrine — the Ship-It-Ugly gate (the soul rule)
doc-ship-ugly.plain: Ship it ugly. Use it daily. Success = “the card was used yesterday” — NOT “the card is complete.”
doc-ship-ugly.barstool: The last thirty versions died of polish. So the rule is blunt: ship it ugly, use it daily, and the only question that counts each morning is — was the card used yesterday? Not “is it pretty.” Not “is it done.” Used.
doc-ship-ugly.boardroom: The Ship-It-Ugly gate inverts the usual quality sequence: a working card in daily use outranks a polished card awaiting completion. The binary success metric — “the card was used yesterday” — gates all expansion: no second card and no new feature until one card has been used live for consecutive days.
doc-ship-ugly.swiss: Gate: Ship-It-Ugly. Metric: binary — card used yesterday (yes/no); an N-consecutive-days counter gates expansion. Documented failure mode: perfect-and-decorate. Verbatim: “Ship it ugly. Use it daily. Success = ‘the card was used yesterday’ — NOT ‘the card is complete.’”

meta.source: doctrine.html §6 / the soul rule, BLUEPRINT header

flypaper-explainer.title: The Flypaper — what is a Cloud Butterfly?
flypaper-explainer.plain: Cloud Butterflies are ideas, insights, and inspirations that come from No Man’s Land — the space between sleep and waking. Catch them here before they fly away.
flypaper-explainer.barstool: You know that genius idea you had in the shower that was gone by breakfast? This is the sticky paper. Dump it here, half-asleep, typos and all.
flypaper-explainer.boardroom: A zero-friction intake for pre-conscious insight capture, feeding the HB1000 innovation pipeline’s scoring and triage stages.
flypaper-explainer.swiss:
PURPOSE — pre-conscious insight capture
WINDOW — No Man’s Land (sleep ⇄ waking)
PROTOCOL — v4.0 · /400 self-score
COUNTERS — Day · Today · YTD (machine-owned)

meta.authored_by: verify (agent-authored per FLYPAPER-LANDING-SPEC §5, pending Tim's review)
meta.source: FLYPAPER-LANDING-SPEC.md §5 — identical to the inline fallback shipped in flypaper.html / library.html (registry block wins at load)

tm-01.title: Timbit tm-01 — It's expensive to be poor
tm-01.plain: It's expensive to be poor — and that's the crime we fix.
tm-01.barstool: You know what costs the most? Being broke. Bank fees when you have no money. Payday loans. Cheap stuff that breaks and you buy it again. The system charges you extra for being poor. That's not bad luck. That's the design.
tm-01.timbit: The system charges you extra for being poor.
tm-01.boardroom: The gangsterism of poverty. The system is designed to keep people poor. Ruby Red is choosing between groceries, her kid's hockey, and a flat tire. That's not a personal failure — that's a design failure.
tm-01.swiss: Record tm-01 · code J-R (Tim Rants). Principle, verbatim: “It's expensive to be poor — and that's the crime we fix.” Context: The gangsterism of poverty. The system is designed to keep people poor. Ruby Red is choosing between groceries, her kid's hockey, and a flat tire. That's not a personal failure — that's a design failure.

tm-02.title: Timbit tm-02 — The system is designed to keep people poor
tm-02.plain: The system is designed to keep people poor — and we are here to redesign the system.
tm-02.barstool: It's not an accident. The fees, the interest rates, the lack of access — it's all built that way. We're not here to hand out fish. We're here to rebuild the pond so people can fish themselves.
tm-02.timbit: Don't hand out fish. Rebuild the pond.
tm-02.boardroom: Social Impact Capitalism isn't charity. It's a structural intervention. We don't hand out fish. We rebuild the pond.
tm-02.swiss: Record tm-02 · code J-R (Tim Rants). Principle, verbatim: “The system is designed to keep people poor — and we are here to redesign the system.” Context: Social Impact Capitalism isn't charity. It's a structural intervention. We don't hand out fish. We rebuild the pond.

tm-03.title: Timbit tm-03 — We solve everything
tm-03.plain: We solve everything. Not some things. Everything.
tm-03.barstool: We don't get to pick the easy problems. If it's in front of us, it's ours. That's the whole deal. You don't get to say 'that's not my department' when someone needs help.
tm-03.timbit: If it's in front of you, it's yours.
tm-03.boardroom: The HB1000 SIC Solve Everything Team doesn't cherry-pick. If it's in front of us, we solve it. That's the mandate.
tm-03.swiss: Record tm-03 · code J-R (Tim Rants). Principle, verbatim: “We solve everything. Not some things. Everything.” Context: The HB1000 SIC Solve Everything Team doesn't cherry-pick. If it's in front of us, we solve it. That's the mandate.

tm-04.title: Timbit tm-04 — Business as a Force for Good
tm-04.plain: Business as a Force for Good is not a tagline — it's an operating system.
tm-04.barstool: It's not something you put on a poster. It's the filter you run every decision through. Does this make things better? If not, why are we doing it?
tm-04.timbit: Every decision runs through one filter: is this a force for good?
tm-04.boardroom: Every decision, every hire, every product, every meeting — filtered through: is this a force for good? If not, why are we doing it?
tm-04.swiss: Record tm-04 · code J-R (Tim Rants). Principle, verbatim: “Business as a Force for Good is not a tagline — it's an operating system.” Context: Every decision, every hire, every product, every meeting — filtered through: is this a force for good? If not, why are we doing it?

tm-05.title: Timbit tm-05 — The gangsterism of poverty
tm-05.plain: The gangsterism of poverty is real — and silence is complicity.
tm-05.barstool: Poverty isn't bad luck. It's enforced. There are systems that profit from keeping people poor. Saying nothing about it means you're okay with it. We're not okay with it.
tm-05.timbit: Silence about poverty is complicity in it.
tm-05.boardroom: Poverty isn't accidental. It's enforced. The structures that keep people poor are intentional. Naming it is the first act of resistance.
tm-05.swiss: Record tm-05 · code J-R (Tim Rants). Principle, verbatim: “The gangsterism of poverty is real — and silence is complicity.” Context: Poverty isn't accidental. It's enforced. The structures that keep people poor are intentional. Naming it is the first act of resistance.

tm-06.title: Timbit tm-06 — Did you get your Timmy's today?
tm-06.plain: Did you get your Timmy's today?
tm-06.barstool: You brush your teeth every day, right? Culture is the same thing. You don't do it once a year at the offsite. You do it every single day. Did you get yours today?
tm-06.timbit: Culture is a daily vitamin, not an annual retreat.
tm-06.boardroom: Culture is a daily practice, not an annual retreat. You take your vitamins every day. You get your Timmy's every day.

tm-07.title: Timbit tm-07 — Robust Collaboration + Robust Communication
tm-07.plain: Robust Collaboration plus Robust Communication equals Impact.
tm-07.barstool: You want to know the formula? Talk to each other, actually. Work together, actually. That's it. Two things. Impact follows.
tm-07.timbit: Talk + work together = impact. That's the whole formula.
tm-07.boardroom: The formula. Not complicated. Not optional. The two inputs that produce the one output that matters.

tm-08.title: Timbit tm-08 — Tone from the top
tm-08.plain: Tone from the top — everything else is noise.
tm-08.barstool: The team doesn't do what you say. They do what you do. If you're late, they're late. If you're honest, they're honest. The tone comes from the top. Always.
tm-08.timbit: They watch what you do, not what you say.
tm-08.boardroom: Culture doesn't come from HR. It doesn't come from a poster on the wall. It comes from what the leader does when nobody's watching — and everybody's watching.

tm-09.title: Timbit tm-09 — Never ask permission
tm-09.plain: Never ask permission. Just do it. Then tell me.
tm-09.barstool: You don't need my permission. I told you the goal. Go get it. Then tell me what you did. Permission culture is the killer of good work.
tm-09.boardroom: Autonomy is the gift. Explain the end goal, then run with it. Permission culture kills momentum. We don't do permission culture here.

tm-10.barstool: If nobody's having fun, something's wrong. Joy isn't a bonus. It's a signal. When people are joyful, they're doing their best work. When they're not, find out why.
tm-10.timbit: Joy is a leading indicator, not a luxury.
tm-10.boardroom: If it doesn't bring joy, we're doing it wrong. Joy is not a luxury. It's a leading indicator of sustainable performance.

tm-11.plain: Every person deserves dignity. Full stop.
tm-11.barstool: Not the people who've earned it. Not the people who deserve it by your standards. Every person. That's the floor. Not the ceiling. The floor.
tm-11.timbit: Dignity is the floor, not the ceiling.

tm-12.title: Timbit tm-12 — The wet computer between our ears
tm-12.plain: The wet computer between our ears is the most underutilized technology on the planet.
tm-12.barstool: You've got the most powerful computer ever built sitting between your ears. And most of us are running it at maybe 10%. The whole game is figuring out how to run it at 100.
tm-12.boardroom: Human Brilliance 1000. The HB1000 framework starts with the premise that every person in the room is carrying a supercomputer. Our job is to create the conditions where it runs at full capacity.

tm-13.title: Timbit tm-13 — The marriage between human and AI
tm-13.plain: The marriage between human and AI is the most important relationship of our generation.
tm-13.barstool: It's not a competition. It's a marriage. You bring the empathy, the judgment, the values. AI brings the scale, the memory, the speed. Together you're unstoppable. Separately you're both limited.
tm-13.timbit: Human + AI = unstoppable. Human vs AI = both limited.
tm-13.boardroom: Not human versus AI. Not AI replacing human. A marriage — which means both parties have to show up, both have to grow, and both have to be honest about what they bring to the table.

tm-14.title: Timbit tm-14 — Move 37
tm-14.plain: Move 37 is what happens when you stop playing the game everyone else is playing.
tm-14.barstool: AlphaGo made a move that every expert said was wrong. It won the game. The best ideas always look wrong at first. If everyone thinks it's a good idea, it's probably too late.
tm-14.timbit: If everyone agrees it's a good idea, you're probably too late.
tm-14.boardroom: AlphaGo's Move 37 wasn't in any human playbook. It looked wrong. It was genius. The best ideas in this organization will look wrong at first. That's how you know they're right.
tm-14.swiss: Record tm-14 · code J-T (Tim Talks). tm-15.title: Timbit tm-15 — North Star thinking
tm-15.plain: North Star thinking means you navigate by something that doesn't move.
tm-15.barstool: Everything is changing all the time. The only way to navigate is to find something that doesn't change. Your values. Your mission. Your why. That's your North Star. Look up.
tm-15.timbit: When everything moves, navigate by what doesn't.
tm-15.boardroom: Strategy changes. Tactics change. Markets change. The North Star doesn't. When everything is uncertain, you look up. That's what the Prime North Star is for.
tm-15.swiss: Record tm-15 · code J-T (Tim Talks). Principle, verbatim: “North Star thinking means you navigate by something that doesn't move.” Context: Strategy changes. Tactics change. tm-16.title: Timbit tm-16 — Cloud Butterfly
tm-16.plain: Cloud Butterfly — the idea that a thought, properly released, can change the weather on the other side of the world.
tm-16.barstool: A butterfly flaps its wings and somewhere a storm starts. That's how ideas work. One thought, shared at the right moment, can change everything. That's why we write things down.
tm-16.timbit: One thought, shared right, can change everything.
tm-16.boardroom: Ideas have leverage. A single insight, shared at the right moment with the right person, can cascade into something that changes systems. That's why we document everything. That's why Timmyisms exist.
tm-16.swiss: Record tm-16 · code J-T (Tim Talks). tm-17.title: Timbit tm-17 — Your next mistake will be adorable.
tm-17.plain: Your next mistake will be adorable.
tm-17.barstool: You're sitting there worried about messing up. And the fortune cookie — the golden one, on Mother's Day — says: relax. Your next mistake is going to be adorable. Not catastrophic. Not career-ending. Adorable. Like a puppy knocking over a water bowl. Just adorable.
tm-17.timbit: Imperfection is endearing. Your next mistake is already adorable.
tm-17.boardroom: Found inside a golden fortune cookie at Tim's Mother's Day brunch, May 10, 2026. Not a warning. Not a threat. A permission slip. The universe reminding you that imperfection is endearing, not disqualifying. Mistakes are how you know you're trying. And the next one? Already adorable.

tm-18.title: Timbit tm-18 — Good luck is trying to catch up to you.
tm-18.plain: Good luck is trying to catch up to you. Slow down so it can.
tm-18.barstool: You're grinding. You're hustling. You're moving so fast you can barely breathe. And the universe is standing there going — hey, I've got something for you. But you keep running past it. Slow down. Just for a second. Let it catch up.
tm-18.timbit: Stillness is a strategy. Good luck needs a target that isn't moving.
tm-18.boardroom: Second golden fortune cookie from Tim's Mother's Day brunch, May 10, 2026. A gentle rebuke to the hustle culture that mistakes speed for progress. Good luck isn't hiding from you — it's chasing you. The problem is you're running too fast for it to land. Stillness is a strategy.

tm-19.title: Timbit tm-19 — There's no THE way
tm-19.plain: There's my way. There's your way. But there is no THE way.
tm-19.barstool: You know that person who's absolutely certain their way is THE way? Under pressure, under stress, under ego — everyone does it. Your way works for you. My way works for me. But nobody gets to own THE way.
tm-19.boardroom: Nietzsche said it: 'You have your way. I have my way. As for the right way, the correct way, and the only way, it does not exist.' Tim's version: under cognitive load, anxiety, hierarchy, or ego, people mistake their personal way for THE universal way. Watch for it. It's everywhere.
tm-19.swiss: Record tm-19 · code J-B (Timbits). Principle, verbatim: “There's my way. There's your way. But there is no THE way.” Context: Nietzsche said it: 'You have your way. I have my way. tm-20.title: Timbit tm-20 — Your action speaks so loud
tm-20.plain: Your action speaks so loud I can't hear a word you're saying.
tm-20.barstool: You know that person who says all the right things and then does the opposite? We all do it sometimes. But here's the thing — nobody hears the words after a while. They just watch what you do. Your actions are broadcasting 24/7 whether you like it or not.
tm-20.timbit: What you do is louder than anything you'll ever say.
tm-20.boardroom: Classic Tim. What you DO drowns out what you SAY — every single time. The meeting where you said culture matters but checked your phone the whole time? We noticed. The email you sent at midnight after saying 'we respect boundaries'? We noticed. Actions are the loudest thing in any room.

tm-21.title: Timbit tm-21 — The Two Golden Rules
tm-21.plain: Rule #1: People don't care how much you know until they know how much you care. Rule #2: He with the gold gets to make all the rules.
tm-21.barstool: Okay so there are two golden rules. The first one — the good one — says people don't care what you know until they know you care about them. That's it. Lead with care. The second one — the one that runs most of the world — says whoever has the money makes the rules. Same word 'gold.' Two completely different games. One builds people up. One extracts from them. Here's the question: which one is your boss running? Which one are YOU running? Because you're running one of them right now, whether you know it or not.
tm-21.timbit: Same word. Two opposite worlds. Which golden rule are you actually running?
tm-21.boardroom: Same word. Two completely opposite operating systems. Golden Rule #1 is the servant leadership version — the one Tim uses, the one SIC and BAFFG are built on. It's about being heard, being known, being felt. You do your best work on the day you concentrate most on making a difference for your people. The final word — 'care' — is the most significant piece to understand. Golden Rule #2 is the power-first, extraction-first version. The one that built the system Babel Bridge is trying to fix. The one that capitalist capitalists typically use. The contrast tells the whole SIC story: one leads with empathy, the other leads with power. Which golden rule is your organization running on?
tm-21.swiss: Record tm-21 · code J-B (Timbits). Principle, verbatim: “Rule #1: People don't care how much you know until they know how much you care. Rule #2: He with the gold gets to make all the rules.” Context: Same word. Two completely opposite operating systems. Golden Rule #1 is the servant leadership version — the one Tim uses, the one SIC and BAFFG are built on. It's about being heard, being known, being felt. You do your best work on the day you concentrate most on making a difference for your people. The final word — 'care' — is the most significant piece to understand. Golden Rule #2 is the power-first, extraction-first version. The one that built the system Babel Bridge is trying to fix. The one that capitalist capitalists typically use. The contrast tells the whole SIC story: one leads with empathy, the other leads with power. Which golden rule is your organization running on?

tm-22.title: Timbit tm-22 — Transparency is kindness
tm-22.barstool: You think you're being kind by not saying the hard thing. You're not. The kindest thing you can do is be honest. Clearly. Without cruelty, but without hiding it either.
tm-22.boardroom: Timmy's two bits. The most compassionate thing you can do is tell the truth clearly.
tm-22.swiss: Record tm-22 · code J-B (Timbits). Principle, verbatim: “Transparency is kindness.” Context: Timmy's two bits. The most compassionate thing you can do is tell the truth clearly.

tm-23.plain: Be autonomous — explain the end goal, then let me run with it.

tm-23.timbit: Tell me the destination. I'll find the road.
tm-23.boardroom: Timmy's two bits. The instruction manual for working with Tim. And for working with anyone who wants to do their best work.

tm-24.title: Timbit tm-24 — Social Impact Capitalism
tm-24.plain: Social Impact Capitalism — putting a ding in the universe.
tm-24.barstool: You can make money and make a difference at the same time. They're not opposites. The ding in the universe is real. It's Ruby Red's flat tire, fixed. It's her kid's hockey paid for. That's the ding.
tm-24.timbit: Make money. Make a difference. Same thing.

tm-25.title: Timbit tm-25 — Human Brilliance 1000
tm-25.plain: Human Brilliance 1000 — because 1000% is the only setting we know.
tm-25.barstool: We don't do 80%. We don't do 'good enough.' HB1000 means you believe every person in the room is capable of 1000% — and you create the conditions to prove it.
tm-25.timbit: Every person is capable of 1000%. Create the conditions.
tm-25.boardroom: Timmy's two bits. HB1000 is not a team name. It's a standard. It's what we expect of ourselves and what we believe every human is capable of.
